item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really want to reply to the comment above me saying "If it's boring then give some constructive criticism to make it no so boring" but I'll settle for saying that I didn't find this boring and liked the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's boring, so downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah thanks. Didn't notice that. I haven't been on here in AGES.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll correct a repeated sentence. Other than that, it's upvote worthy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The break from science stuff to urban-legend stuff broke it for me. Feathers in the cores from Mercury and blue cheese-smelling cores from the moon just ruined it.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the moon is made of cheese.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There were a bunch of tabs after the [[/>]], and that breaks the formatting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, what happened to the rating module?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The moon is made of cheese? Really?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually liked this, the bit where it started talking about the anomalous effect was a good "wait, what?" moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article would be better with no picture, instead of the stock photo looking thing it has now…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this article. However, after reading "Zen and the art of [Data Expunged]" I feel neccessary for a no vote. Censoring numbers gave no substance to the article for me, and really soured my enjoyment of it.

Overall, it's good, but no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, it doesn't. Ectoentropy revolves around the existence or nonexistence of matter and energy; a movement from a musical piece that shouldn't exist could come from any number of sources. It could be an extradimensional copy, or hell it could just be someone making it up.

Why are you so hell-bent on having this tag in your article when it is just fine without it? There's nothing functionally wrong with the document, it just isn't ectoentropic. There's almost no way you could rewrite this concept in order to make it fit that description, and you're only going to damage the narrative if you try.

Let. It. Go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right, I added a thing which creates non-existent movements of The Planets. Now can it be ectoentropic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I apreciate the clarification, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a general rule, any trait of a SCP is only tag-worthy if it's explicitly mentioned in the article or extremely easily and unambiguously deducible from what is explicit. In your specific example, that would depend on how the article was written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Point of information: Is it absolutely neccessary that the trait be obvious in-universe for the tag to be applied out-of-universe? I'm not too invested either way, but I'm curious as to general policy.

I'm thinking of situations where the anomaly is obviously one thing, to the reader, but presents to the Foundation as something completly different. An object that erases all memories and records of a person inside when it activates. That would look like a device that makes new people, from the point of view of a Foundation scientist. So does it get tagged with "ectoentropic" or "mind affecting"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I created the definitions for those tags, and you haven't provided enough proof in the body of your article to justify that definition. So, again, convince me.

To clarify: You have not proven beyond the shadow of a doubt that this SCP operates in violation of the law of entropy by creating something out of nothing. You are insisting that the material comes from the celestial bodies that you list in your logs, therefore there is teleportation going on, but you do not know whether some of the mass being transported is being transmuted en-route or actually being created whole cloth. Hell, for all we know, there's an extraterrestrial gremlin that's just stuffing all that random shit into the transfer mechanism just to fuck with our heads.

The point is, you haven't made it clear that actual ectoentropic generation is taking place, and just like the article that involves a GoI that is in the author's headcanon but never explicitly named, this isn't tag-worthy. Either fix the article or stop fucking with the tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Page tags are part of site infrastructure, and are not open to author creative license. There is an objective definition of each tag that has to apply to the article for it to be valid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's ectoentropric because where do you think the feathers, and the musical scores come from? Ectoentropic stays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the author is saying it's ectoentropic, I'm not sure it's our place to argue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do you keep adding the ectoentropic tag back in? I've already explained why it doesn't fit, so you need to either convince us that it belongs or leave it off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[22:13] <Aelanna> We don't know where they're coming from, but we can't rule out that some of the mass teleported from that particular celestial body was transmuted into it.
[22:13] <Aelanna> But the language of the document implies that we know without a doubt that most of the material is extraterrestrial in origin.
[22:13] <Aelanna> Thus by insufficient evidence, I'm maintaining that ectoentropic is inappropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but it's not fully polished yet.

-One or two minor spelling errors ("sample retrieved weas consistent", for example), but I'm the last guy I'd trust with fixing typos.
-"Breeze blocks" - is that a technical term? It jumped at me as too informal, but it might just be because I'm not familiar with the terminology
-"glovebox" - same as above, but I'm a bit more certain that it's not a properly clinical term
-What Mr. Wilt said. There is no reason to conclude that these are cores retrieved versus created from nothing.

I like to imagine that these are not sampling the physical planets, but rather the collective conceptualization of them. The core concepts, if you'll forgive a terrible pun. Hence the music and cheese and other oddities. Because most of the population thinks of mars, venus, etcetera the way NASA reports them, but some portion still think in terms of the old folk tales or similar romantic notions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No. The blue is a reference to 'once in a blue moon'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is there only a single-digit chance of getting molten sulfur from Io? Isn't "molten sulfur" kind of Io's thing?

Better phrasing for the bit about temperatures in the main body - "samples retrieved were consistent with available data concerning the average surface temperature of Venus." It's hard to have something that's "exactly the same" as an average value, aside from the phrasing not being normative for the Foundation.

I did like the occasional retrieval of movements from Holst. The other anomalies (aside from the feathers from Mercury) could use some work. And I thought the Moon was supposed to be made of green cheese?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd added it due to the notes and the feathers and cheese-scented blue rocks that occasionally appear when it's drilling. We don't know for certain where those are coming from, and I really doubt there's actually cheese rocks on the moon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't ectoentropic; we clearly know where the stuff is coming from. Tag removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a few issues with this. Overall, I'm not fond of the concept. It just doesn't do anything for me. While I originally would have just neutral voted, I found there were a few errors that, overall, drove me to downvote it. How do we know these are from their respective planets? A good deal (if not most) of what we do know about the composition of our system's planets is theoretical. While these are educated and reasonable theories, we don't know for certain what all of the planets and their moons are made from. Additionally, how do we know that these are, in fact, coming from their respective planets and not being created? Sure there's labels for each drill, but if one can make blue-colored cheddar cheese, how do we know it's not just creating the samples according to what's believed about the composition of the planets? It's demonstrating the ability to create matter itself, after all, so it wouldn't be an unreasonable assumption to make.

I also found your sentence structuring and general organization of the article to be weird. A lot of the sentences seem overly terse, and others seem to transition poorly. I wouldn't say that it's not following tone, it just all seems grammatically and structurally off. You also repeat this sentence twice:

Occasionally, samples retrieved by SCP-1426 bits will show properties not present on some of the bodies that said samples are cored from (see Addendum-1426-1).

Overall, I'm not really sure what you could do to improve it. The only suggestion that stands out to me is to try to straighten out your science and proof a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat idea, and feathers on Mercury was my favorite part - but it hinted at how good this article could be - and drew attention to the fact that the other planets' samples, separately or as a whole, were pretty dull. This needs more cultivation. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely a fan of the edits, especially the bit about the Planets. Gives it a layer of mystery that the original notes were lacking. You've got my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this version better than the draft I saw- it's got a nice depth to it, and a certain weirdness that's a bit funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also no-voting for this reason.

*MOD PANTS ON MODE* But please note that it is against the rules to downvote for this reason, as it has to do with the actions of the author instead of the content of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idea: Perhaps the blank pages could instead show unseen musical compositions relating to their respective celestial bodies in the style of Gustav Holst, even though they clearly weren't written by Holst?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the redacted percentages are extremely silly, and think the random-accident-followed-by-discovery thing is a bit cliche, but otherwise like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I…actually really like that change. It was unexpected, but I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removed the notes and replaced them with a Gustav Holst reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe you could give us a hint of its origin and/or its original purpose, if you think it would make it interesting, but this is just my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. Still not hooking me. Sorry that this isn't really specific, but the notes aren't doing much for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've added a part which says that the name of the bodies is etched into the bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My advice is to get rid of the goofier ones, like the aforementioned "[EXPLETIVE REDACTED]" one. If you're really trying to convey horror through recovered notes, it's better to be subtle about it (for instance, a note that just cut off mid sentence) rather than hitting the audience over the face with "SOMETHIN BAD HAPPENED".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should I remove the notes? Maybe replace it with something different? Can someone give me a few ideas?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, it still doesn't do much for me. It's not bad, I just don't like it enough to upvote. Therefore, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The notes really kill it for me. They are random, interchangeable nothings that lack any kind of substance. There's no reason for [EXPLETIVE REDACTED] either, unless this is something playing a joke on the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that's pretty shady. I was going to upvote because I liked the concept a lot, and wanted to get you out of the negative.

Now you've already done 15 times my part for yourself.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Understandable. But doing stuff like that looks like you're trying to evade negative consequences/downvotes. This is your first warning, and don't do it again or Mods/Admins will have to take action.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry. I wanted a fresh start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the basic idea, but I think there are some gaps in the logic, most notably how the heck did the foundation identify which solar bodies these cores came from? I also think it would be better off without the notes - they're a pretty cliche addendum to a pretty unique concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait. You deleted and immediately reposted this in an effort to clear out downvotes? Even if this is a revised version, that's still a pretty big no-no violation, author.

Also: still not interesting to me. Meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really like this. It's trying to be all mysterious ,but I can't see the connection between the drill bit texts and well, anything else, plus trapped in a whatever in Pi digits was done by XKCD already.

Also, I have no idea how the Foundation can tell what location on what planetoid the samples came from. I mean, allright, we know the composition of moon rock, and a few other planets, but it changes around the surface just like earths' and with Jupiter's moons it feels like a wild mass guess.
I'm not a geologist, so I might be wrong, but that means at the very least this should have been explained a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it but the notes seemed a little bit if an add on for creep effort. Didn't seem to fit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok. Now this is interesting. But I am having trouble trying to comprehend the anomaly. Is it the drill or is it the properties extracted?

Testing logs might also help this but the changes were good. I give this a +1 for effort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just there in the drill bit when you pull it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the changes +1

A little question though: How the hell would you get a note from drilling?
Would it appear in the dust or something?
